title: Mali: Food Security Outlook Update - Reduced food access worsening acute food insecurity in the north and center, April - September 2026 (pdf)
summary: Mali - Food Security Outlook Update April - September 2026  Emergency (IPC Phase 4) outcomes observed in the Ménaka  region will continue through September due to the severe  difficulties populations face in accessing food, linked to the  major deterioration of livelihoods and the high number of  displaced persons. Reduced food access worsening acute food insecurity in the north and center  Key Messages  This report provides an update to the February to September 2026  Food Security Outlook and March 2026 Key Message Update. Crisis (IPC Phase 3) outcomes will persist  in Kidal through September 2026 due to degraded livelihoods.
bullets: Mali - Food Security Outlook Update April - September 2026 Emergency (IPC Phase 4) outcomes observed in the Ménaka region will continue through September due to the severe difficulties populations face in accessing food, linked to the major deterioration of livelihoods and the high number of displaced persons.

Reduced food access worsening acute food insecurity in the north and center Key Messages This report provides an update to the February to September 2026 Food Security Outlook and March 2026 Key Message Update.
Crisis (IPC Phase 3) outcomes will persist in Kidal through September 2026 due to degraded livelihoods.

In other insecure areas, food insecurity outcomes ranging from Stressed (IPC Phase 2) to Crisis (IPC Phase 3) will continue through September 2026 for poor households whose purchasing power has been severely eroded.

Coordinated attacks by Jama’at Nusrat al-Islam wal-Muslimin (JNIM) and the Azawad Liberation Front (FLA) on Mopti, Gao, Kidal, Kati, and Bamako on April 25, as well as the blockade of Bamako announced by JNIM on April 28, are disrupting trade flows.

full_text:
Reduced food access worsening acute food insecurity in the north and center 
Key Messages 
This report provides an update to the February to September 2026 
Food Security Outlook and March 2026 Key Message Update. The 
analysis is based on information available as of April 28, 2026. 
Mali - Food Security Outlook Update April - September 2026 
Emergency (IPC Phase 4) outcomes observed in the Ménaka 
region will continue through September due to the severe 
difficulties populations face in accessing food, linked to the 
major deterioration of livelihoods and the high number of 
displaced persons. Crisis (IPC Phase 3) outcomes will persist 
in Kidal through September 2026 due to degraded livelihoods. 
In other insecure areas, food insecurity outcomes ranging from 
Stressed (IPC Phase 2) to Crisis (IPC Phase 3) will continue 
through September 2026 for poor households whose purchasing 
power has been severely eroded. 
The resurgence of security incidents in the north and 
center between the army and armed groups continues to trigger 
displacement and further degrade household livelihoods. 
Coordinated attacks by Jama’at Nusrat al-Islam wal-Muslimin 
(JNIM) and the Azawad Liberation Front (FLA) on Mopti, Gao, 
Kidal, Kati, and Bamako on April 25, as well as the blockade of 
Bamako announced by JNIM on April 28, are disrupting trade 
flows. FEWS NET is monitoring the ongoing road blockages that 
are hindering trade flows and could affect acute food insecurity 
in Bamako if prolonged, and will update its analysis in May. 
Disruptions to transhumance are causing an early 
deterioration in livestock conditions in the northern part of 
the country, reducing livestock production and pastoral income, 
thereby weakening the purchasing power of pastoral 
households. 
Disruptions in fuel supply, particularly diesel, are expected 
to continue due to developments in the Middle East and 
insecurity along the Dakar and Abidjan corridors, leading to 
higher transportation costs and an increased cost of living. This 
situation limits poor households’ access to food, forcing them to 
rely on borrowing, support from relatives and friends, and 
significant reductions in non-food and even food expenditures. 
FEWS NET is a United States Government-funded activity. The content of this report does not
necessarily reflect the view of the United States Government.
FEWS NET Mali
mali@fews.net
fews.net/west-africa/mali
Current anomalies in food security conditions as of April 2026 
Security incidents continue to be reported across the country, 
particularly in the northern, western, and central regions, involving 
clashes between the army and the armed groups JNIM, FLA, and the 
Islamic State in the Greater Sahara (EIGS). Coordinated attacks by 
JNIM and FLA in the regions of Kidal, Gao, Mopti, and the cities of 
Kati and Bamako against military positions on April 25, 2026 
significantly disrupted population movements, especially in Kidal. 
On April 28, JNIM announced a blockade of Bamako, reducing 
commercial flows into the capital. Armed groups are intensifying 
repression against civilian populations. Restrictions on freedom of 
movement, combined with population displacement, are worsening 
already precarious household conditions. 
Disruptions to transhumance movements caused by insecurity 
are leading to an early deterioration of livestock conditions in 
areas with high concentrations of herds in the north of the country, 
creating feed shortages for livestock. In addition to reducing herd 
sizes, these difficulties are causing animal production (milk, butter, cheese) to fall below typical levels. The decline in 
pastoral income is limiting the purchasing power of pastoral households. 
Insecurity continues to restrict access to certain fishing areas in the Niger Delta and in Tombouctou and Gao, 
reducing catches and, consequently, incomes to below average levels, while also weakening the purchasing power of 
fishing households in these areas. 
Market supply disruptions persist in insecure areas of the center and north due to attempted blockades and 
the payment of illegal taxes to armed groups, particularly in the regions of Ménaka, Kidal, Gao, and Tombouctou. 
Access to staple cereals in these areas remains limited for poor households because prices remain above the five-
year average by 16 percent in Gao (millet), 25 percent in Ménaka (millet), and 37 percent in Kidal (wheat semolina) in 
March (Figure 1). 
Persistent difficulties related to fuel availability have worsened due to disruptions in maritime trade linked to 
the conflict in the Middle East. At the end of March, the government increased gasoline prices by 13 percent and 
diesel prices by 30 percent amid an ongoing supply crisis within the country, and prices on parallel markets range 
from double to even higher than official rates. In Kidal, gasoline sells for 2,500 FCFA per liter and diesel for 1,500 
FCFA per liter, compared with official prices of 875 and 940 FCFA, respectively. The country continues to suffer the 
impacts of trade disruptions related to the situation in the Middle East, given its heavy dependence on international 
fuel imports. Transport costs increased by more than 30 percent compared with March, driving up the prices of 
essential goods (food and non-food), and further reducing the purchasing power of poor households in a context of 
declining income-earning opportunities. Following the increase in transport costs and the fees paid to armed groups 
in Kidal at the end of March, the price of imported rice increased from 40,000 to 65,000 FCFA per 50 kilogram bag. 
The combination of the electricity crisis and increasing fuel prices continues to negatively affect the 
country’s economy. Although fuel availability has improved compared with the previous three months due to the 
absence of attacks on fuel convoys since March, supplies remain limited outside Bamako. Rising production costs for 
industrial units and increasingly mechanized agricultural operations are slowing economic activities, further 
limiting employment and income-earning opportunities for poor households, particularly in urban centers and areas 
most affected by the fuel crisis. 
Major disruptions to maritime trade in the Middle East, a major supplier of liquefied natural gas, have 
increased fertilizer costs on the global market. According to the agricultural service, fertilizer prices rose between 
February and March 2026 by 19 percent for urea, 18 percent for NPK, and 8 percent for DAP, reducing poor farmers’ 
access to these inputs. At the end of March 2026, the country’s available mineral fertilizer stock stood at 146,366 
Figure 1 
Price of staple cereals (millet, maize, 
sorghum, semolina), March 2026 
Source: Food and Nutrition Security Monitoring and 
Early Warning Agency 
Food Security Outlook Update April - September 2026
Famine Early Warning Systems Network 2
metric tons, representing only 22 percent of the estimated needs of 676,646 metric tons. Reduced access to these 
essential inputs for rice, maize, and cotton cultivation during the typical supply period for the new agricultural season 
beginning in May is expected to negatively affect anticipated production. A government campaign, supported by 
technical partners, is underway to promote the use of locally produced organic fertilizers to mitigate impacts on 
agricultural production. 
Humanitarian food assistance 
Food assistance funding, amounting to 4.5 million USD since the beginning of 2026, has covered 3 percent of needs 
(OCHA); comparable levels had been observed in April 2025, but at that time covered only 1.8 percent (OCHA). 
During the first quarter of 2026, food assistance reached only about 9.7 percent (approximately 117,000 people) of 
the target population (1.2 million people), according to the Food Security Cluster. This level is significantly lower 
than in a typical year before the contraction of humanitarian funding in 2025; around 491,000 beneficiaries had been 
recorded during the same period in 2024. Difficulties in mobilizing humanitarian funding have reduced the level of 
coverage of energy needs, with only 241 000 people planned to receive food rations covering between 50 and 100 
percent of needs during the pastoral lean season from March to June 2026 
Updates to key assumptions about atypical food security conditions through September 2026 
Most of the assumptions that underpinned FEWS NET’s analysis of the most likely scenario for the Mali Food 
Security Outlook from February to September 2026 remain valid; however, the following updates have been made 
to incorporate new evidence: 
Security incidents are expected to persist throughout the country, while evolving conflict dynamics will 
increase pressure on the capacities and efforts of Malian authorities to ensure security along major 
transport corridors. Armed groups are expected to continue strategies aimed at multiplying tension hotspots 
across the country through attacks against armed forces positions, civilian populations, and increasingly against 
industrial facilities. 
The increase in official gasoline and diesel prices will raise industrial and agricultural production costs, 
as well as transportation costs. Although an improvement in fuel supply has been observed compared with 
previous months, the impact of the Middle East crisis will worsen the fuel crisis that has been ongoing in Mali 
since September 2025 through higher prices and reduced availability caused by maritime trade disruptions. 
Prices of staple cereals, which were generally stable at the end of March compared with February, are expected 
to rise following the fuel price increase at the beginning of April. Roadblocks established by armed groups on 
routes leading to Bamako will reduce trade flows and prolong disruptions in the capital. Disruptions in the 
country’s fuel supply will persist, with the crisis particularly affecting diesel countrywide. 
Tensions in international markets and rising transportation costs will further reduce imported fertilizer 
volumes and keep fertilizer prices high on the domestic market. Mali’s main fertilizer suppliers include 
Russia and Morocco, through Senegal and Côte d’Ivoire. According to the FAO, the continuation of the crisis is 
expected to contribute to an average increase in global fertilizer prices of 15 to 20 percent during the first half 
of 2026, coinciding with the country’s typical fertilizer procurement period (January to May). This increase is 
expected to result in further fertilizer price hikes in Mali (a net importer of these products) during the critical 
preparation phase for the 2026/27 agricultural season. Higher fertilizer prices will reduce import volumes. 
Reduced access to fertilizers essential for food crops (rice, maize, millet, sorghum) and cash crops 
(cotton) is expected to lead to lower agricultural production and fewer agricultural labor opportunities, 
thereby reducing rural household incomes in the context of atypically high food prices. 
According to the April update of seasonal forecasts, cumulative rainfall is expected to be average to above 
average across the country, while below-average rainfall is likely in southwestern Mali. Reduced rainfall 
could negatively affect production prospects in this part of the country, although good spatiotemporal rainfall 
distribution may mitigate the impact of this deficit. 
Food Security Outlook Update April - September 2026
Famine Early Warning Systems Network 3
Projected acute food insecurity outcomes through September 2026 
In northern Mali, Emergency (IPC Phase 4) outcomes in Ménaka and Crisis (IPC Phase 3) in Kidal will persist 
through September during the pastoral lean season. The resurgence of insecurity continues to disrupt the local 
economy and market supply chains. Household access to food is significantly reduced due to food prices that remain 
far above the five-year average in Kidal (37 percent) and Ménaka (25 percent). The sharp decline in pastoral income, 
particularly in Ménaka where herd sizes have been drastically reduced following excessive livestock sales and theft, is 
further worsening the situation. The ongoing early pastoral lean season will intensify the seasonal deterioration of 
food consumption due to atypically low availability of animal products and reductions in the volume and quality of 
meals. Poor households are unable to access food without resorting to negative coping strategies such as distress 
livestock sales, begging, and dependence on assistance. The already precarious nutritional situation in Kidal and 
concerning conditions in Ménaka (severe acute malnutrition prevalence of 3.4 percent according to the October 2025 
ENSAN survey) will persist due to the atypical worsening of food consumption, challenges in accessing health and 
social services, and limited humanitarian assistance. 
Despite similar security shocks, food security outcomes are more severe in Ménaka, where households have endured 
the prolonged effects of conflict, massive displacement (more than 20 percent of the population), and persistent 
inaccessibility of some areas. In Kidal, households still retain some assets due to the relatively recent intensification 
of attacks observed since last year. However, a small proportion of displaced households and poor households in 
Kidal — mainly displaced persons and poor households in inaccessible areas — will face Emergency (IPC Phase 4) 
or worse outcomes. Security incidents occurring since April 25 are not expected to significantly alter acute food 
insecurity outcomes in these regions at this stage. FEWS NET will continue to monitor the situation, particularly in 
Kidal. 
In insecure areas of Gao, Mopti, and Tombouctou, particularly the districts along the border zone with Burkina 
Faso and Niger known as the “Liptako Gourma,” ongoing Stressed (IPC Phase 2) outcomes will deteriorate to Crisis 
(IPC Phase 3) from May through September 2026. The early depletion of food stocks will result in household 
market dependence for longer than normal, while incomes decline in the face of high food prices. Difficulties in 
accessing markets are exacerbating households’ reliance on strategies such as sharply reducing non-food and food 
expenditures, borrowing, and selling productive assets to meet food needs. Despite some income and food from 
agricultural activities, gathering, and animal products (milk, butter) in June, food consumption will further deteriorate 
through September due to reductions in the number, quantity, and quality of meals. The already concerning 
nutritional situation in these areas is expected to worsen beyond typical levels for the same period. Poor displaced 
households and refugees in these zones, who no longer have regular access to sharply reduced humanitarian 
assistance, will remain in Crisis (IPC Phase 3) to Emergency (IPC Phase 4) through September 2026. Monitoring of 
market functioning, obstacles to humanitarian assistance and economic activities at the start of the agricultural 
season, and population movements linked to recent developments will be strengthened to update the food security 
situation in these areas. 
In the northern areas of the Ségou, Nara, and Nioro regions, ongoing Stressed (IPC Phase 2) outcomes are 
expected to continue through September 2026. A small proportion of the population, particularly displaced 
persons and the poorest households, will face Crisis (IPC Phase 3). Declining incomes linked to insecurity and the 
fuel crisis, worsened by the conflict in the Middle East, will reduce households’ ability to meet food and non-food 
Economic activities, including off-season and rainy season agricultural activities from February to 
September, will be affected by supply difficulties as well as rising fuel and fertilizer prices. Areas where 
agricultural mechanization is highly developed, such as the cotton-producing zones in the south of the country 
and irrigated areas in the Niger River Valley, will be particularly affected. Reduced production is expected, 
especially for crops with high fertilizer requirements such as rice, maize, and cotton. 
According to the World Bank, disruptions in national food production, rising food import costs, and higher 
hydrocarbon costs are expected to fuel inflation in oil-importing countries. As a net importer, Mali will likely 
experience these inflationary pressures. 
Food Security Outlook Update April - September 2026
Famine Early Warning Systems Network 4
needs in a disrupted economic environment. The early lean season, caused by the premature depletion of food 
stocks and high food prices, will reduce food consumption compared with a typical year. Poor households will rely on 
reducing non-food expenditures, selling livestock, and increasing agricultural labor activities, reliance on solidarity 
networks, and borrowing. 
In urban centers, ongoing Stressed (IPC Phase 2) outcomes will continue through September 2026 due to 
declining economic activity linked to the electricity crisis and the fuel crisis worsened by the conflict in the Middle 
East. Rising living costs are eroding household purchasing power and reducing access to food. Poor households are 
resorting to borrowing, support from relatives and friends, and major reductions in non-food and even food 
expenditures. In Bamako, the announced blockade could significantly reduce the flow of goods, including staple 
foods, from the rest of the country into the capital, creating a medium-term risk of price increases if disruptions to 
trade flows persist. This would further weaken the purchasing power of poor households whose incomes are already 
declining due to the economic slowdown. FEWS NET is monitoring developments and will update its analysis in May. 
Although the agricultural zones of the south are expected to remain in Minimal (IPC Phase 1) through 
September 2026, declining purchasing power — resulting from reduced agricultural and non-agricultural incomes 
compared with a typical year — will limit adequate food access for a proportion of households. These households are 
expected to face Stressed (IPC Phase 2) conditions from June through September 2026. 
Annex 1: Updates to events that would likely change projected acute food insecurity outcomes 
While FEWS NET’s projections are considered the “most likely” scenario, there is always a degree of uncertainty in the 
assumptions that underpin the scenario. This means food security conditions and their impacts on acute food security may 
evolve differently than projected. FEWS NET issues monthly updates to its projections, but decision makers need advance 
information about this uncertainty and an explanation of why things may turn out differently than projected. As such, the 
final step in FEWS NET’s scenario development process is to briefly identify key events that would result in a credible 
alternative scenario and significantly change the projected outcomes. FEWS NET only considers scenarios that have a 
reasonable chance of occurrence. 
National 
A sharper increase in global oil and fertilizer prices linked to the conflict in the Middle East 
Likely impact on acute food insecurity outcomes: In this scenario, extremely high global fertilizer and fuel prices 
are expected to lead to a sharper increase in the prices of these products in Mali, while also limiting their availability. 
Although a further official increase in fuel prices is unlikely under this alternative scenario, it is expected that reduced 
fuel supply linked to high global prices will result in a sharp rise in informal fuel prices, as well as a general 
deterioration in fuel availability countrywide. The simultaneous increase in production costs for industrial units and 
agricultural operations will slow economic activities, further limiting employment and income-earning opportunities 
for poor households, particularly in urban centers, who will resort to atypical coping strategies to meet their food and 
non-food needs. The limited availability and significant price increases will further restrict producers’ access to 
fertilizers essential for food crops, thereby reducing off-season rice production more than normal, as well as the 
cultivated areas for the new agricultural campaign. Reduced off-season production and increased transport costs will 
drive up staple food prices, limiting poor households’ access to food. Their purchasing power will decline from June to 
September 2026, during the agropastoral lean season, which will begin early (as soon as May) in insecure areas. As a 
result, an increase in the number of people facing Crisis (IPC Phase 3) or worse conditions — and even a deterioration 
from Stressed (IPC Phase 2) to Crisis (IPC Phase 3) — would likely occur in insecure areas that are highly dependent 
on markets for food consumption (Gao, Nara, Mopti, and Bandiagara). 
Food Security Outlook Update April - September 2026
Famine Early Warning Systems Network 5
Kidal Region 
A military operation to retake Kidal occurs in May 
Likely impact on acute food insecurity outcomes: The army’s decision to return to Kidal would trigger violent 
clashes with armed groups. Major disruptions to economic activities and market supply chains, as well as increasing 
food prices, would reduce households’ ability to meet their food and non-food needs more than previously 
anticipated. Difficulties in accessing food and humanitarian assistance would increase the number of people in need. 
Under this scenario, outcomes in Kidal would deteriorate from Crisis (IPC Phase 3) to Emergency (IPC Phase 4) 
starting in May, in a region where economic opportunities are already extremely limited and populations are highly 
dependent on markets. 
Annex 2: Updates to key sources of evidence used in this analysis 
Many of the key sources of evidence utilized for FEWS NET’s February to September 2026 Food Security Outlook 
remain the same; however, new and additional sources of evidence are listed below. 
Insecurity/conflict Africom Qualitative Evolution of conflicts and intensity of security 
incidents

incidents/blockades on trade flows and

market

Food Security Outlook Update April - September 2026
Famine Early Warning Systems Network 6
Annex 3: Seasonal calendar 
Recommended citation: FEWS NET. Mali Food Security Outlook Update April - September 2026: Reduced food access 
worsening acute food insecurity in the north and center, 2026. 
Food Security Outlook Update 
This Food Security Outlook Update provides an analysis of current acute food insecurity conditions and any changes to FEWS NET's latest projection of acute food insecurity outcomes 
in the specified geography over the next six months. Learn more here. 
Source: FEWS NET 
* FEWS NET classification is IPC-compatible with limited exceptions. IPC-compatible analysis follows key IPC protocols but does not necessarily reflect the consensus of national food security 
partners. As of IPC 3.0, the IPC no longer assesses the impact of food assistance on classification and thus no longer maps the (!). However, FEWS NET continues to produce food security 
maps inclusive of the (!) as well as maps compatible with IPC 3.0/3.1, which include the mapping of food security assistance bags. In rare cases, FEWS NET classification is IPC-aligned but 
not IPC-compatible. IPC-aligned analysis is evidence-based but some types of evidence required for IPC-compatibility were not available. 
Food Security Outlook Update April - September 2026
Famine Early Warning Systems Network 7

related.title: Aligning the U.S. and Canadian Defense Industrial Bases
related.summary: The United States and Canada are both racing to rebuild their defense industrial bases, recognizing that future conflicts will be determined not only by military capability, but  by the ability to produce at scale . After decades of  reliance on globalized supply chains  for everything from consumer products to critical defense technologies, the United States is reasserting a more active industrial policy, using tools ranging from the  Defense Production Act  to  incentivizing private capital  investments and  even selective government equity stakes . For many countries that have pursued efficiency-driven globalization for decades, the economic pull of China’s market makes unilateral disengagement difficult, even when national security concerns are clear.
bullets: The United States and Canada are both racing to rebuild their defense industrial bases, recognizing that future conflicts will be determined not only by military capability, but by the ability to produce at scale .

After decades of reliance on globalized supply chains for everything from consumer products to critical defense technologies, the United States is reasserting a more active industrial policy, using tools ranging from the Defense Production Act to incentivizing private capital investments and even selective government equity stakes .

For many countries that have pursued efficiency-driven globalization for decades, the economic pull of China’s market makes unilateral disengagement difficult, even when national security concerns are clear.
Luckily, for many countries, allied markets represent a significantly larger combined economic opportunity than China alone.

The challenge, therefore, is not simply one of dependence, but of working together through aligned demand, investment, and industrial policy to create markets and demand signals that can compete with the gravitational pull of China’s economy.
related.excerpt: The United States and Canada are both racing to rebuild their defense industrial bases, recognizing that future conflicts will be determined not only by military capability, but by the ability to produce at scale . But they cannot succeed alone — and importantly, they do not need to start from scratch. After decades of reliance on globalized supply chains for everything from consumer products to critical defense technologies, the United States is reasserting a more active industrial policy, using tools ranging from the Defense Production Act to incentivizing private capital investments and even selective government equity stakes . Canada is undergoing a parallel shift, with increased defense spending commitments, the recent release of its first Defence Industrial Strategy , and the newly launched Defence Investment Agency . These efforts create significant opportunity, but also carry risk. While the United States and Canada already share deep and often underappreciated industrial integration, in some sectors, this collaboration is applied late or unevenly. Without more deliberate alignment early in the process, the two countries may duplicate investments, compete for scarce labor a

related.title: Western Withdrawal, Jihadist Expansion: How the Sahel Became Ground Zero for Global Terrorism
related.summary: The United States completed its  withdrawa l  from Niger in September 2024, abandoning key drone and intelligence hubs after its military government terminated their bilateral agreement. On April 25, armed groups launched near-simultaneous attacks against military installations and key strategic sites across Mali. French forces  withdrew  from Mali in 2022 after the military junta demanded its exit, ending Operation Barkhane.
bullets: The United States completed its withdrawa l from Niger in September 2024, abandoning key drone and intelligence hubs after its military government terminated their bilateral agreement.
On April 25, armed groups launched near-simultaneous attacks against military installations and key strategic sites across Mali.
French forces withdrew from Mali in 2022 after the military junta demanded its exit, ending Operation Barkhane.

The systematic breakdown of military and diplomatic relationships with Sahelian states has left Western actors increasingly unable to see, shape, or contain events in what has become the world’s most lethal theatre of jihadist violence.
From Intervention to Expulsion Over the past three years, Western military presence and influence across the central Sahel have collapsed.
related.excerpt: On April 25, armed groups launched near-simultaneous attacks against military installations and key strategic sites across Mali. Claimed by Jama’at Nusrat al-Islam wal-Muslimin , a jihadist group, and conducted in coordination with Tuareg separatist forces from the Front de libération de l’Azawad, the attacks targeted multiple nodes across the country’s security architecture simultaneously, from the capital Bamako to Gao, Mopti, and Kidal. While the attacks themselves were a shock, they should be understood as the logical endpoint of a deteriorating security trajectory that Western governments have watched from an ever-increasing distance. The geographic scope of the attacks, the targeting of senior officials, and the coordination between jihadist and separatist forces reflect a step change in insurgent capability that has been building for years. Western governments now face a predicament of their own making. As the regional security environment has worsened, their capacity to respond has diminished in direct proportion. The systematic breakdown of military and diplomatic relationships with Sahelian states has left Western actors increasingly unable to see, shape, or contain event

related.title: Israeli security minister stirs diplomatic outrage with flotilla activist abuse video
related.summary: Israeli minister Ben-Gvir posts video taunting detained Gaza flotilla activists – video Italy’s prime minister, Giorgia Meloni, said the images were “unacceptable”, and demanded the release of all Italian citizens involved, along with an apology for the mistreatment and the display of “total contempt” toward the Italian government. “We condemn the actions of Israeli minister Ben-Gvir – who Australia has sanctioned – and the degrading actions of Israeli authorities towards those detained.”  New Zealand’s foreign affairs minister, Winston Peters, instructed the ministry to call in the Israeli ambassador so his “grave concerns” could be directly passed on. Israel’s far-right national security minister, Itamar Ben-Gvir, has sparked a diplomatic crisis by publishing footage of Israeli security forces abusing international activists who were detained as they tried to sail to Gaza with aid.
bullets: Israeli minister Ben-Gvir posts video taunting detained Gaza flotilla activists – video Italy’s prime minister, Giorgia Meloni, said the images were “unacceptable”, and demanded the release of all Italian citizens involved, along with an apology for the mistreatment and the display of “total contempt” toward the Italian government.

“We condemn the actions of Israeli minister Ben-Gvir – who Australia has sanctioned – and the degrading actions of Israeli authorities towards those detained.” New Zealand’s foreign affairs minister, Winston Peters, instructed the ministry to call in the Israeli ambassador so his “grave concerns” could be directly passed on.

Israel’s far-right national security minister, Itamar Ben-Gvir, has sparked a diplomatic crisis by publishing footage of Israeli security forces abusing international activists who were detained as they tried to sail to Gaza with aid.
“The team reports systemic violations of due process, and widespread physical and psychological abuse by Israeli authorities,” the rights group Adalah said in a statement.
The US ambassador to Israel, Mike Huckabee, one of the country’s staunchest allies, described Ben-Gvir’s behaviour as “despicable” and said the minister had “betrayed the dignity of his nation”.
related.excerpt: Israel’s far-right national security minister, Itamar Ben-Gvir, has sparked a diplomatic crisis by publishing footage of Israeli security forces abusing international activists who were detained as they tried to sail to Gaza with aid. Three activists were taken to hospital as result of Israeli violence, lawyers representing the group said. They were subsequently discharged. Dozens of others have suspected broken ribs, resulting in breathing problems. “The team reports systemic violations of due process, and widespread physical and psychological abuse by Israeli authorities,” the rights group Adalah said in a statement. There were “a large number of complaints of extreme violence”. Ben-Gvir’s video drew a rapid and furious response from countries whose citizens were onboard the boats, including the UK, Canada, Germany, the Netherlands, Spain, Ireland, Australia and New Zealand, in many cases from the top of government. The US ambassador to Israel, Mike Huckabee, one of the country’s staunchest allies, described Ben-Gvir’s behaviour as “despicable” and said the minister had “betrayed the dignity of his nation”. related.excerpt: KYIV, Ukraine — Senior White House officials publicly placed Ukraine’s military ahead of allied counterparts in Europe across four venues last week, and in some respects ranked Kyiv ahead of the United States itself, even as U.S. President Donald Trump has continued to dismiss Ukraine’s military strength. The Ukrainian armed forces are “the strongest, most powerful armed forces in all of Europe,” Secretary of State Marco Rubio said last week, citing a five-to-one Russian-to-Ukrainian casualty rate and four years of battlefield adaptation. The necessity of fighting the war, Rubio said, has pushed Ukrainians to develop “new tactics, new techniques, new equipment, new technology that is creating a sort of hybrid asymmetrical warfare.” U.S. Army Secretary Dan Driscoll told the Senate Armed Services Committee the same week that Ukraine has fused drones, sensors and weapons into a single command network running across the front, while U.S. Army systems remain “compartmentalized, isolated and ineffective against modern threats.” “Ukraine’s Delta common operating system, their modular open system architecture command and control system, is absolutely incredible,” Driscoll testified. “It fu

related.title: Ukraine and Russia Are Souring on U.S. Negotiations
related.summary: Yet the Trump administration has in some ways pulled its punches when it comes to Kyiv: Although it has decreased the volume of aid it gives to Ukraine, it still sells weapons to the country via NATO, provides Kyiv with intelligence support, and sanctions Russian oil (though those sanctions have been  partially lifted ). Last August, Russia triumphantly presented a summit with the United States in Alaska as a breakthrough in its war to conquer Ukraine, later  waxing poetic  about the “spirit of Anchorage.” Almost a year later, that spirit is gone. In March, Lavrov  said  the spirit of Anchorage was evaporating; and in April, he said  negotiations  were not Moscow’s “top priority.” “It’s pretty clear they’re down on [negotiations],” said John Herbst, senior director of the Atlantic Council’s Eurasia Center.

On the U.S. side, negotiations have been led first by Steve Witkoff, a real estate developer and friend of U.S. President Donald Trump, and later jointly by Witkoff and Jared Kushner, Trump’s son-in-law. related.excerpt: Senior military special operations forces leaders from the U.S., Sweden and the Philippines participated in a morning panel today about the importance of SOF partnerships and alliances, as part of Special Operations Forces Week 2026 in Tampa, Florida. During the one-hour conversation, the four participants discussed how building trust through international alliances enhances collective readiness and interoperability, making it a prerequisite for mission success in contested environments. "I think the most important thing is to continue to build trust between partners within the alliance, [and] also between partners," Swedish army Brig. Gen. Rickard Johansson, commander of Sweden's special operations forces, replied after the panel was asked what the international SOF community needs to focus on most to ensure that alliances are stronger, truly integrated and capable of meeting the challenges of an increasingly complex and contested world. "And we will continue to do that, because that is the bottom line for all activities that we are supposed to do: build trust," Johansson added. Air Force Maj. Gen. Claude K. Tudor Jr., commander of U.S. Special Operations Command Africa, concurred

related.title: Calls to Uphold International Law Dominate Security Council Debate on Civilian Protection, as Delegates Demand Safety for Journalists, Aid Workers
related.summary: While civilians continue to suffer because parties to conflict choose to ignore their legal obligation to protect them, the Security Council heard today that adhering to the rules of war — and enforcing accountability when they are broken — can produce a different result. Decade after Resolution 2286 (2016), Medical, Humanitarian Workers under Increasing Attack In 2025 alone, she noted, the UN recorded more than 1,350 attacks on medical care across 18 conflicts. Just yesterday, 19 May, the organ met following attacks on civilian populations in  Ukraine  and infrastructure in the  United Arab Emirates .

Rather, it results from the choice made by conflicting parties to ignore their obligations to protect civilians, to adopt increasingly permissive interpretations of international law, to subordinate the protection of civilians to claims of military necessity or to allow impunity prevail.
The Council is holding its annual day-long open debate on the protection of civilians in armed conflict as the United Nations observes the ninth iteration of its “ Protection of Civilians Week ”.
related.excerpt: While civilians continue to suffer because parties to conflict choose to ignore their legal obligation to protect them, the Security Council heard today that adhering to the rules of war — and enforcing accountability when they are broken — can produce a different result. The Council is holding its annual day-long open debate on the protection of civilians in armed conflict as the United Nations observes the ninth iteration of its “ Protection of Civilians Week ”. Just yesterday, 19 May, the organ met following attacks on civilian populations in Ukraine and infrastructure in the United Arab Emirates . “One civilian was killed approximately every 14 minutes in 2025,” said Edem Wosornu, Director of the Crisis Response Division in the Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs (OCHA). All too often, civilians are not collateral damage — “they are the target”, she stressed. Explosive weapons tear through towns and cities, destroying lives and the infrastructure that sustains them. And healthcare is also under attack, as she observed that — 10 years after the adoption of resolution 2286 (2016) on protecting medical care in conflict — “the situation has only gotten worse”. related.title: Italy rethinks EU defense-financing aid as arms spending falls out of fashion
related.summary: Italian prime minister Giorgia Meloni has told EU Commission chief Ursula von der Leyen that Italy may pass on the offer of €14.9 billion in cheap, so-called SAFE loans from Brussels which could prove essential to pushing Italian defense spending towards NATO’s five percent of GDP target. In a letter to Von der Leyen on Sunday, Meloni said it was imperative to use state cash to soften the blow of rising energy costs — and the consequent hike in the cost of living — caused by the current slowdown of oil supplies to Italy due to the blockage of the Strait of Hormuz. Italy’s defense minister Guido Crosetto said the final decision to use or not use SAFE loan needed to be taken by the end of May, and the last word needed to come from Italy’s finance ministry.
bullets: Italian prime minister Giorgia Meloni has told EU Commission chief Ursula von der Leyen that Italy may pass on the offer of €14.9 billion in cheap, so-called SAFE loans from Brussels which could prove essential to pushing Italian defense spending towards NATO’s five percent of GDP target.

In a letter to Von der Leyen on Sunday, Meloni said it was imperative to use state cash to soften the blow of rising energy costs — and the consequent hike in the cost of living — caused by the current slowdown of oil supplies to Italy due to the blockage of the Strait of Hormuz.
Italy’s defense minister Guido Crosetto said the final decision to use or not use SAFE loan needed to be taken by the end of May, and the last word needed to come from Italy’s finance ministry.
He said he had written twice to finance minister Giancarlo Giorgetti asking what Giorgetti planned to do, but had yet to receive a response.
Meloni will nevertheless need to explain to NATO partners why she is considering passing on EU plans to boost defense spending at the next alliance summit in Turkey on July 7-8.
related.excerpt: ROME — Italy is considering backing out of a second EU-supported boost to defense spending, taking to about €27 billion ($31 billion) the amount of rearmament cash it could forego as it focuses on tackling soaring energy costs. Italian prime minister Giorgia Meloni has told EU Commission chief Ursula von der Leyen that Italy may pass on the offer of €14.9 billion in cheap, so-called SAFE loans from Brussels which could prove essential to pushing Italian defense spending towards NATO’s five percent of GDP target. Italy’s defense minister Guido Crosetto said the final decision to use or not use SAFE loan needed to be taken by the end of May, and the last word needed to come from Italy’s finance ministry. He said he had written twice to finance minister Giancarlo Giorgetti asking what Giorgetti planned to do, but had yet to receive a response. The suggestion by Meloni is the second time in two months she has publicly mulled passing on an EU-driven way to push up spending. In April she suggested Italy will not take advantage of an EU scheme allowing Rome to exempt a quantity of defense spending from calculations of annual deficit spending in order to allow extra arms purchases without
